---
title: "Use R in your Dagster pipelines with `{dagsterpipes}`"
description: "Learn how R can now be used seamlessly within Dagster projects."
author: "Joe Kirincic"
date: "2026-02-15"
categories: [R,dagster,data-engineering]
image: "thumbnail.png"
image-alt: ""
draft: true
---

## Introduction

I'm a big fan of Dagster for managing data pipelines, but I'm also a huge fan of R for writing data pipelines, training machine learning models, and so on. Recently, I got tired of not them not being able to combine well together, so I set out to fix that. These efforts resulted in a new R package called [`{dagsterpipes}`](https://github.com/joekirincic/dagsterpipes). In this post, I'm going to show how you can now leverage R inside of Dagster using the package.

## Prerequisites

To follow along with this post, you should understand the following.

-   Dagster: what it is, how to set it up., etc.
-   Docker

## What we're building

We're going to create a new asset in our Dagster project that uses R for processing the data. Specifically, we're going to have an asset that does some basic ETL taking data from a database and plopping it into a new database table. Apart from being able to materialize a Dagster asset written in R, we also want R to communicate progress of the run with the Dagster instance. We also want to display metadata related to the run when the asset is done materializing.

What we're building needs to materialize an asset using R code. In addition, we want what we've built to be able to communicate with Dagster so we get the same observability we love about it.

## Dagster Pipes Protocol

In 2024, Dagster announced their new Pipes protocol for language agnostic workflows (TODO: check date). Maybe you have some legacy Java service that updates data in a warehouse; maybe the job is written in Spark, or in our case, R. The Dagster Pipes Protocol allows Dagster to communicate with any language that implements the protocol. So to make R runnable inside of Dagster, we need an implementation of the Pipes protocol for R. That's where the package `{dagsterpipes}` comes in; it provides the Pipes protocol implementation we need. The specifics of how it achieves this aren't as important as what it enables. Let's see some R code running in Dagster.

## Our first R-based asset

Our R asset is going to update a table inside of our Postgres database. First, it runs a SQL query to extract some data, and if there's rows, it drops the relevant records in the target table and inserts the rows returned from the SQL query. Consider the R code below.

```{r, eval=FALSE}

library(dagsterpipes)
library(DBI)

ctx <- open_dagster_pipes()

ctx$log("Starting ETL process.")

ctx$log("Fetching data from DB.")

con <- dbConnect(
  drv = RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = Sys.getenv("DB_NAME"),
  host = Sys.getenv("DB_HOST"),
  port = Sys.getenv("DB_PORT"),
  user = Sys.getenv("DB_USER"),
  password = Sys.getenv("DB_PASSWORD")
)

df <- mtcars

ctx$log("Fetched data from DB.")

dbWriteTable(con, name = "mtcars", value = df, overwrite = TRUE)

ctx$log("Finished ETL process.")

ctx$report_asset_materialization(
  metadata = list(
    row_count = pipes_metadata_value(nrow(df), "int")
  )
)

ctx$log("Process completed.")

ctx$close()
```

The big thing here is the `ctx` variable we get from calling `open_dagster_pipes`. The context is where Dagster shoves information related to the run like partition keys and other environment variables. On the R-side, we can log information about the run that gets sent back to Dagster and shown inside of the run details page. How does this look on the Python side? Pretty simple. Consider the code below.

```{python, eval=FALSE}

import dagster as dg
from pathlib import Path

R_ETL_SCRIPT = Path(__file__).parent.joinpath("etl.R")

@dg.asset()
def r_asset(context: dg.AssetExecutionContext, pipes_subprocess_client: dg.PipesSubprocessClient):
    return pipes_subprocess_client.run(
        command=["Rscript", str(R_ETL_SCRIPT)],
        context=context
    ).get_materialize_result()
    
```

The most important thing to note in this code is a new Dagster resource we're passing as argument to the asset function, a `PipesSubprocessClient` object. This object simply invokes `Rscript <OUR_R_SCRIPT>`.

## Conclusion

Now your Dagster projects can benefit from the powerful facilities throughout the R ecosystem. Go nuts.
